title: Every Homo naledi we know of is female, and the implications are fascinating
gist: Protein analysis of dental enamel from 23 Homo naledi teeth in Rising Star Cave revealed that all known specimens are genetically female, a ~0.000095% probability by chance, strongly supporting intentional sex-segregated burial practices in a small-brained hominin 236,000–335,000 years ago. This evidence compels a reassessment of cognitive and cultural complexity in pre-modern hominins and raises ethical questions about excavating the graves of what may constitute a non-human but culturally sophisticated species. Paleoproteomics is emerging as a key tool for reconstructing hominin evolution where ancient DNA is unavailable.

themes.theme: Paleoproteomics as a Breakthrough Analytical Method

insights.claim: Dental enamel protein analysis (paleoproteomics) successfully determined biological sex across 23 Homo naledi teeth from Rising Star Cave, demonstrating the method's power in cases where ancient DNA has degraded beyond sequencing — opening a new analytical frontier for human evolutionary science.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
entities: Max Planck Institute for Evolutionary Anthropology
insights.so_what: Paleoproteomics represents an emerging scientific methodology with commercial and academic investment implications for companies developing protein-based ancient biomolecule analysis tools.
insights.quote: Max Planck Institute for Evolutionary Anthropology molecular scientist Palesa Madupe and her colleagues recently analyzed the proteins in dental enamel from 23 Homo naledi teeth unearthed in four different cave chambers
insights.locator: Little bits of teeth had a big surprise

insights.claim: Amelogenin protein isoforms AMELX (X-chromosome) and AMELY (Y-chromosome) in dental enamel provide reliable sex determination in ancient hominins, with male individuals expected to contain roughly one-tenth as much AMELY as AMELX — a ratio detectable even in degraded ancient samples.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
insights.so_what: The robustness of protein-based sex determination in ancient teeth validates paleoproteomics as a high-confidence tool, potentially driving demand for mass-spectrometry and proteomics lab services in paleontology and forensics.
insights.quote: In genetically male humans, dental enamel usually contains about a tenth as much AMELY as AMELX, and Madupe and her colleagues say that given the level of AMELX in their samples, they should have been able to detect AMELY if it was there.
insights.locator: Little bits of teeth had a big surprise

insights.claim: Dentin (the material inside teeth) contains more proteins than enamel and could offer richer paleoproteomics data, but extracting it requires cutting deeper into rare fossils — posing an ethical and methodological tradeoff that will shape the pace of discovery in ancient human origins research.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
insights.so_what: The tradeoff between destructive sampling and analytical richness is a bottleneck for the field, creating opportunities for non-destructive imaging and analytical technologies.
insights.quote: Dentin, the material inside teeth, contains more proteins than enamel, which means it might offer more answers—but at the cost of damaging rare and irreplaceable fossils.
insights.locator: What else do Homo naledi's proteins tell us?

insights.claim: Researchers at University of Copenhagen aim to expand protein sequencing across South African, Kenyan, Tanzanian, and Ethiopian hominin fossils to build a molecular hominin family tree — a project that could take many years and significant lab investment.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
insights.so_what: A pan-African paleoproteomics initiative signals sustained institutional investment in protein-based evolutionary research infrastructure.
insights.quote: "That's the aspiration, to start to have molecular evidence that is rich enough to be informative for reconstruction of early hominin evolution in Africa and outside Africa," Cappellini tells Ars Technica.
insights.locator: What else do Homo naledi's proteins tell us?

themes.theme: Statistical Evidence for Intentional Sex-Segregated Burial in Homo naledi

insights.claim: The probability that all 20+ Homo naledi individuals at Rising Star Cave are female by random chance is approximately 0.000095%, equivalent to flipping a coin 20 times and landing on the same side each time — making accidental sex clustering statistically implausible.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
insights.so_what: The statistical near-impossibility of coincidental all-female grouping forces acceptance of intentional behavior, reshaping scientific consensus on non-human cognition and cultural capacity.
insights.quote: The odds of that being a coincidence are about the same as the odds of flipping a coin 20 times and landing on the same side every time: 0.0000954 percent
insights.locator: Little bits of teeth had a big surprise

insights.claim: Even infant and baby Homo naledi remains in Rising Star Cave are all female, ruling out the alternative hypothesis that adult females alone were venturing into caves for ritual or foraging activity — because male children would be expected in a co-traveling group.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
insights.so_what: The inclusion of female infants closes the last behavioral loophole and confirms intentional female-only burial as the most parsimonious explanation.
insights.quote: Even so, the group inevitably includes children of both sexes. But even the babies in Rising Star are all female.

insights.claim: University of Wisconsin paleoanthropologist John Hawks states the all-female burial pattern at Rising Star represents "clear evidence of culture" — and asserts there is no other process that could produce the observed demographic pattern.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
insights.so_what: A prominent co-author's categorical statement signals emerging scientific consensus, likely accelerating publication and funding for follow-on research.
insights.quote: "It is rarely the case that we have such clear evidence of culture as this case is," University of Wisconsin paleoanthropologist John Hawks, another co-author of the recent study, tells Ars Technica. "It's like, there is no other process that can make this happen."

themes.theme: Reassessing Cognitive Complexity in Small-Brained Hominins

insights.claim: Homo naledi, with a brain roughly the size of a chimpanzee's (~450cc), appears to have practiced intentional sex-segregated burial, used fire, and created engravings — collectively suggesting that complex cultural behavior does not require large brain size, challenging a long-held assumption in paleoanthropology.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
insights.so_what: This fundamentally disrupts the brain-size-to-culture correlation that underpins many models of cognitive evolution, with broad implications for AI and cognitive science research framing.
insights.quote: its brain is about the size of a chimpanzee's, but it's shaped and organized a lot like ours
insights.locator: What else do Homo naledi's proteins tell us?

insights.claim: Homo naledi left engravings on Rising Star Cave walls that were described in a 2023 paper — markings that Lee Berger argues were not intended for Homo sapiens but for the hominins themselves or their descendants, implying symbolic communication or memory practices.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
insights.so_what: Evidence of symbolic engraving in a small-brained hominin extends the origin of abstract thought deeper into prehistory than previously accepted.
insights.quote: "Those symbols weren't meant for us. Whatever they're meant for, it certainly wasn't for a Homo sapiens," he says. "They were meant either for them to come back to, their descendants to come back to, or some other purpose, but they weren't meant for us."

insights.claim: The combination of burial, fire use, and engravings in Homo naledi — a species with a chimpanzee-sized brain — suggests culture may have originated earlier in the hominin lineage than previously thought, with brain organization (not size alone) being the critical variable.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
insights.so_what: This reframes assumptions about the prerequisites for cultural behavior, with potential spillover implications for AI research on the relationship between architecture and capability.
insights.quote: There is a divide in the field between those that think that humans evolved from cultural species that were before us, and those that believe that culture originated with modern humans

themes.theme: The Unknown Male Homo naledi and Fossil Misclassification Risk

insights.claim: No Homo naledi male has ever been positively identified; males may be significantly larger than females, meaning previously dismissed or unclassified larger hominin fossils from other sites could in fact be male Homo naledi — a systematic misclassification risk in the paleontological record.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
insights.so_what: Potential large-scale fossil misclassification means the Homo naledi lineage's geographic range and diversity may be substantially underestimated, reshaping the known hominin family tree.
insights.quote: "I do think that there are other fossils from other sites that we have previously ruled out as naledi, in some cases, because they're bigger than naledi," he says.

insights.claim: Prior to the protein analysis results (received in 2024), the Homo naledi research team believed the species showed almost no sexual dimorphism because all skeletons looked similar in size — this assumption was entirely wrong, illustrating how protein sex-determination data can overturn morphology-based conclusions.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
insights.so_what: Demonstrates that paleoproteomics can falsify long-held morphological interpretations, validating investment in molecular over morphological methods for ancient species analysis.
insights.quote: "Our study helps resolve the long-standing mystery of why Homo naledi lacked significant variation," says Madupe in a recent press release about the study. "It's probably because they could have all belonged to one sex."

themes.theme: Scientific Community Resistance to Non-Human Culture Claims

insights.claim: A persistent divide exists in paleoanthropology between researchers who believe culture originated with Homo sapiens and those who see it as a trait inherited from earlier hominins — and this ideological split causes systematic resistance to accepting evidence of culture in species like Homo naledi regardless of evidence quality.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
insights.so_what: Scientific paradigm resistance is a structural feature of the field; breakthroughs like Rising Star's protein data will face prolonged debate before shifting consensus, extending the timeline for mainstream acceptance.
insights.quote: "There is a divide in the field between those that think that humans evolved from cultural species that were before us, and those that believe that culture originated with modern humans," says Hawks, "so they resist any claims of culture earlier unless they have some sort of extraordinary evidence."

insights.claim: Despite growing evidence of Neanderthal art and abstract thought, the scientific debate about Neanderthal cognitive capacity remains active — illustrating a broader pattern where evidence for non-human sophistication accumulates far faster than consensus changes.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
insights.so_what: Consensus lag in paleontology means high-impact discoveries like Rising Star may take 5–10 years to be fully integrated into textbooks and funding priorities.
insights.quote: There's an ongoing debate about Neanderthal art and abstract thought despite a growing pile of evidence.

themes.theme: Ethics of Excavating Culturally Sophisticated Non-Human Species

insights.claim: Lee Berger and his team have paused excavations at Rising Star Cave following the all-female protein results, signaling a shift from treating Homo naledi as a fossil species to treating it as a culturally significant entity deserving ethical consideration before further disturbance.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
insights.so_what: If the field moves toward ethical constraints on excavating culturally sophisticated hominins, it could reshape research permit frameworks, museum collection policies, and institutional research strategies.
insights.quote: Homo naledi's protein results, with their grave implications (not sorry), prompted Berger and his team to pause their excavations in the cave system, although analysis in the lab continues.

insights.claim: Lee Berger frames Homo naledi as humanity's "first contact with a non-human species" that had culture, art, and burial practices — and argues this requires a new ethical framework distinct from both the treatment of human ancestors and the treatment of fossils.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
insights.so_what: A new ethical category for culturally sophisticated non-human species could create regulatory and legal precedents affecting how research institutions handle ancient human relative remains globally.
insights.quote: "This is our first contact with a—and I think it's important to repeat this—a non-human species. Their brains are not human brains," says Berger.

insights.claim: Berger states that Homo naledi's cultural evidence obligates scientists to "stop digging hominins like dinosaurs" — suggesting that the field may need to develop ethical standards closer to those used for human archaeological sites than for paleontological excavations.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
insights.so_what: Regulatory and ethical shifts in excavation practices could slow research timelines and increase costs for paleoanthropology projects globally.
insights.quote: "It certainly will mean we have to stop digging hominins like dinosaurs," Berger says.

themes.theme: Ancient DNA Prospects and Technological Improvement in Sequencing

insights.claim: A decade ago, attempts to sequence Homo naledi DNA from Rising Star bones failed due to degradation from South Africa's heat; since then, newly unearthed bones may be better preserved, and improved sampling and sequencing technologies have revived the prospect of ancient DNA extraction.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
insights.so_what: Rapid improvements in ancient DNA sequencing technology are bringing previously inaccessible fossil genomes within reach — a trend benefiting genomics and lab-services companies serving research institutions.
insights.quote: "We are assessing whether it's now promising to do DNA sampling, and I am hopeful," says Hawks. "I can't yet predict what outcome we might get."
insights.locator: What else do Homo naledi's proteins tell us?

insights.claim: Homo naledi's 236,000–335,000-year-old remains in South Africa's warm climate represent a harder challenge for ancient DNA recovery than the cold, dry environments where Neanderthal and Denisovan DNA was successfully sequenced — demonstrating that climate is a primary determinant of ancient DNA survival.
insights.speaker: Kiona N. Smith
insights.so_what: Cold-storage and cryopreservation technologies in archaeology and paleontology may become increasingly valued as researchers attempt to extend DNA recovery to warmer-climate fossil sites.
insights.quote: South Africa's heat breaks down DNA faster and more thoroughly than where those other remains were found.
insights.locator: What else do Homo naledi's proteins tell us?

top_entities: Max Planck Institute for Evolutionary Anthropology
